description: Expanded Egyptian mythology with comprehensive deities, stories, places, motifs, and relationships.

mythologies.name: Egyptian Mythology
mythologies.summary: The religious beliefs and narratives of ancient Egypt, centered on the gods, the afterlife, and the cosmic order (maat).
mythologies.overview: Egyptian mythology encompasses creation myths, the solar cycle, the judgment of the dead, and the maintenance of cosmic order through ritual.
mythologies.worldviewSummary: Cyclical renewal; pharaoh as divine; maat (order) versus isfet (chaos); death as transition to the afterlife.
mythologies.cosmologySummary: Sky goddess Nut arches over earth god Geb. Sun god travels by day, underworld at night. Duat is the realm of the dead.

beings.name: Ra
beings.summary: Sun god, creator, king of the gods.
beings.originSummary: Emerges from Nun (primordial waters); creates the world.

beings.name: Osiris
beings.summary: God of the dead, resurrection, and fertility; first king and judge of the afterlife.
beings.originSummary: Murdered by Seth; resurrected by Isis; becomes lord of the underworld.

beings.name: Isis
beings.summary: Goddess of magic, healing, and motherhood; wife of Osiris, mother of Horus.
beings.originSummary: Assembles Osiris and conceives Horus; protects the sun god from Apophis.

beings.name: Horus
beings.summary: Falcon god of kingship and the sky; son of Osiris and Isis; avenges his father.
beings.originSummary: Conceived after Osiris's death; battles Seth for the throne.

beings.name: Seth
beings.summary: God of chaos, storms, and the desert; killer of Osiris; opponent of Horus.

beings.name: Anubis
beings.summary: Jackal god of mummification and the dead; guides souls and weighs the heart.
titles: He Who Is Upon His Mountain

beings.name: Thoth
beings.summary: God of writing, wisdom, and the moon; recorder of the judgment of the dead.

beings.name: Bastet
beings.summary: Cat goddess of protection, fertility, and domestic harmony.

beings.name: Sekhmet
beings.summary: Lioness goddess of war, plague, and healing; destructive aspect of Ra.

beings.name: Ptah
beings.summary: Creator god of craftsmen and architects; god of Memphis.

beings.name: Nut
beings.summary: Sky goddess who arches over the earth; mother of the main gods.

beings.name: Geb
beings.summary: Earth god lying beneath Nut; father of the main gods.

beings.name: Hathor
beings.summary: Goddess of love, music, dance, and motherhood; often depicted as a cow.

beings.name: Amun
beings.summary: Hidden god of Thebes; later merged with Ra as Amun-Ra.

beings.name: Mut
beings.summary: Mother goddess of Thebes; consort of Amun and mother of Khonsu.

beings.name: Khonsu
beings.summary: Moon god of time and healing; son of Amun and Mut in Thebes triad.

beings.name: Maat
beings.summary: Goddess of truth, justice, and cosmic order; feather used in judgment.

beings.name: Apep (Apophis)
beings.summary: Giant serpent of chaos; enemy of Ra who attacks the solar barque nightly.

beings.name: Sobek
beings.summary: Crocodile god of the Nile, military, and fertility.

beings.name: Wepwawet
beings.summary: Jackal god of war and funerals; 'Opener of the Ways'.

beings.name: Nekhbet
beings.summary: Vulture goddess of Upper Egypt; protectress of pharaoh.

beings.name: Wadjet
beings.summary: Cobra goddess of Lower Egypt; protectress of pharaoh.

beings.name: Khnum
beings.summary: Ram-headed god who creates humans on his potter's wheel.

beings.name: Taweret
beings.summary: Hippopotamus goddess of childbirth and fertility.

beings.name: Bes
beings.summary: Dwarf god of domestic protection, music, and childbirth.

stories.name: Death and Resurrection of Osiris
stories.summary: Seth murders Osiris; Isis reassembles and revives him; Horus is conceived and later avenges his father.
stories.description: Seth tricks Osiris into a coffin and dismembers him. Isis gathers the pieces and, with magic, conceives Horus. Osiris becomes lord of the dead. Horus and Seth contest the throne.

stories.name: Judgment of the Dead
stories.summary: The deceased's heart is weighed against Maat's feather; Anubis and Thoth oversee the judgment.
stories.description: In the Hall of Two Truths, the heart is weighed against the feather of maat. If the heart is light, the soul joins Osiris. If heavy, it is devoured.

stories.name: Heliopolitan Creation
stories.summary: Atum emerges from primordial waters and creates the first divine pair.
stories.description: From Nun (primordial waters), Atum emerges and creates Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture). They give birth to Geb (earth) and Nut (sky), who then produce Osiris, Isis, Seth, and Nephthys.

stories.name: Solar Journey Through Duat
stories.summary: Ra travels through the underworld nightly, battling Apep to ensure sunrise.
stories.description: Each night, Ra sails his solar barque through the twelve hours of Duat, facing various challenges and battling Apep the chaos serpent. His victory ensures the sun rises again.

stories.name: Isis's Quest for Osiris
stories.summary: Isis searches Egypt for Osiris's dismembered pieces and restores him.
stories.description: After Seth murders and dismembers Osiris, Isis travels throughout Egypt gathering the pieces. She uses magic to restore Osiris long enough to conceive Horus.

stories.name: Contest Between Horus and Seth
stories.summary: Eighty-year struggle between Horus and Seth for the throne of Egypt.
stories.description: Horus and Seth contest for rulership through battles, transformations, and divine judgments. Eventually, Horus is declared rightful king of Egypt.

stories.name: Ra's Battle with Apep
stories.summary: Daily battle between Ra and chaos serpent Apep in the underworld.
stories.description: Each night in Duat, Ra battles Apep who tries to swallow the solar barque. With help from other gods, Ra defeats Apep, ensuring sunrise.

stories.name: Bastet and Sekhmet
stories.summary: Dual nature of the feline goddess as both gentle protector and fierce warrior.
stories.description: The feline goddess manifests as gentle Bastet and fierce Sekhmet, representing the dual nature of protection and destruction, domestic harmony and warfare.

places.name: Duat
places.summary: The underworld; realm of the dead and nightly journey of the sun.
places.description: Ra travels through the Duat at night; the dead undergo judgment and dwell with Osiris.

places.name: Heliopolis
places.summary: Sacred city of the sun god Ra; center of solar worship.
places.description: Major religious center where Ra was worshipped and the Heliopolitan creation myth developed.

places.name: Memphis
places.summary: Ancient capital; cult center of Ptah and creator god worship.
places.description: First unified capital of Egypt, center of Ptah worship and creator god theology.

places.name: Thebes
places.summary: Great city of Amun; center of the Theban Triad worship.
places.description: Capital during New Kingdom, center of Amun worship and the Theban Triad (Amun, Mut, Khonsu).

places.name: Abu Simbel
places.summary: Rock temples of Ramses II; monument to divine kingship.
places.description: Massive rock temples demonstrating pharaoh's divine status and connection to the gods.

places.name: Abydos
places.summary: Sacred city of Osiris; center of afterlife beliefs.
places.description: Major cult center of Osiris, believed to be his burial place and gateway to the afterlife.

places.name: Karnak
places.summary: Vast temple complex of Amun-Ra; largest religious complex in Egypt.
places.description: Massive temple complex dedicated to Amun-Ra, representing the divine palace on earth.

places.name: Luxor Temple
places.summary: Temple of divine kingship and Opet Festival celebrations.
places.description: Temple celebrating the divine birth and renewal of the pharaoh during the Opet Festival.

motifs.name: Death and Rebirth
motifs.summary: Cyclical pattern: death leads to renewal, as with Osiris and the solar cycle.

motifs.name: Maat (Cosmic Order)
motifs.summary: Divine principle of order, truth, and justice maintaining cosmic balance.
motifs.description: Maat represents the cosmic order that must be maintained through proper behavior, ritual, and royal governance. Chaos (isfet) threatens when maat is violated.
motifs.interpretation: Foundation of Egyptian moral and cosmic order, essential for civilization's survival.

motifs.name: Solar Cycle
motifs.summary: Daily journey of the sun through day, night, and renewal.
motifs.description: Ra travels across the sky by day, through Duat at night, and is reborn each morning. This cycle represents the pattern of life, death, and renewal.
motifs.interpretation: Cosmic model for human existence and the promise of renewal after death.

motifs.name: Divine Kingship
motifs.summary: Pharaoh as living god maintaining maat between gods and humans.
motifs.description: The pharaoh embodies Horus on earth and becomes Osiris after death, serving as the crucial link maintaining cosmic order through proper governance and ritual.
motifs.interpretation: Political theology justifying royal authority and cosmic responsibility.

motifs.name: Magic and Protection
motifs.summary: Use of magical words, symbols, and rituals for protection and healing.
motifs.description: Magic (heka) is a divine force used for protection, healing, and maintaining order. Names, symbols, and spells provide power against chaos and evil.
motifs.interpretation: Egyptian belief in the power of words and symbols to affect reality and provide protection.

motifs.name: Divine Duality
motifs.summary: Complementary opposites creating cosmic balance and completeness.
motifs.description: Egyptian thought emphasizes complementary pairs: Upper and Lower Egypt, order and chaos, male and female, life and death. These opposites create dynamic balance rather than conflict.
motifs.interpretation: Philosophical understanding of cosmic balance through complementary forces.

groups.name: Ennead of Heliopolis
groups.summary: Nine gods of the Heliopolitan creation myth.

groups.name: Ogdoad of Hermopolis
groups.summary: Eight primordial deities representing chaos before creation.

groups.name: Theban Triad
groups.summary: Divine family of Amun, Mut, and Khonsu worshipped in Thebes.

objects.name: Ankh
objects.summary: Symbol of life, held by gods.

objects.name: Eye of Horus (Udjat)
objects.summary: Symbol of protection, healing, and restoration.

objects.name: Crook and Flail
objects.summary: Symbols of royal authority and divine kingship.
objects.symbolicMeaning: Royal authority, divine kingship, shepherd leadership

objects.name: Canopic Jars
objects.summary: Containers for preserving organs during mummification.

objects.name: Scarab Beetle
objects.summary: Symbol of rebirth and the rising sun.

relationships.description: Horus defeats Seth in their contest

sources.title: Book of the Dead
sources.description: Funerary texts for the judgment and afterlife journey.

sources.title: Coffin Texts
sources.description: Funerary spells and afterlife guides for non-royal Egyptians.

sources.title: Hermopolitan Cosmogony
sources.description: Creation myths from the Ogdoad tradition.

sources.title: Ramesside Love Songs
sources.description: Poetic texts about love and divine relationships.

sources.title: Westcar Papyrus
sources.description: Stories about magic and divine intervention.
